questions.title: Myocardial Infarction
questions.text: A client is admitted to the emergency department with suspected myocardial infarction. Which electrocardiogram (ECG) change would confirm this diagnosis?
questions.rationale: ST segment elevation on an ECG is the hallmark finding of an acute myocardial infarction (specifically, an ST-elevation myocardial infarction or STEMI), indicating acute injury to the myocardium. This elevation occurs in leads corresponding to the anatomical area of the heart that is experiencing infarction. ST segment depression can indicate ischemia but not necessarily infarction, or it may be seen in a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI). Tall, peaked T waves are often seen in hyperkalemia, not myocardial infarction. U waves may be present in hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, or with certain medications, but are not characteristic of myocardial infarction.

questions.title: Cardiac Medications
questions.text: A nurse is administering nitroglycerin to a client with chest pain. Which action by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.text: Have the client swallow the tablet with water
Instruct the client to place the tablet under the tongue
Apply the tablet topically to the chest

questions.rationale: Sublingual nitroglycerin tablets should be placed under the tongue where they dissolve and are rapidly absorbed into the circulation, providing quick relief of chest pain. The client should be instructed not to swallow, chew, or drink water with the tablet, as this would reduce its effectiveness. Nitroglycerin is not administered with food, as this would delay absorption. While nitroglycerin is available in topical forms (patches and ointments), these are used for prophylaxis rather than acute treatment of chest pain, and they are applied to hairless areas of the skin, not specifically to the chest.

questions.title: Cardiac Arrhythmias
questions.text: A client's cardiac monitor shows a regular rhythm with a rate of 50 beats per minute, P waves present before each QRS complex, and a PR interval of 0.20 seconds. The nurse interprets this as:
questions.rationale: First-degree AV block is characterized by a prolonged PR interval (greater than 0.20 seconds) with a P wave before each QRS complex and a regular rhythm. In sinus bradycardia, the heart rate is less than 60 beats per minute with normal PR interval (0.12-0.20 seconds). Though the scenario describes a rate of 50 beats per minute (which is bradycardic), the prolonged PR interval of 0.20 seconds indicates first-degree AV block. Second-degree AV block, Mobitz I (Wenckebach) features progressively prolonged PR intervals until a P wave fails to conduct, resulting in a dropped QRS complex. A junctional rhythm typically has absent or inverted P waves, or P waves that occur after the QRS complex.

questions.title: Heart Failure Management
questions.text: A client with heart failure is prescribed furosemide (Lasix). Which assessment finding would indicate that the medication is effective?
questions.rationale: Furosemide (Lasix) is a loop diuretic that increases urine output by inhibiting sodium and chloride reabsorption in the kidneys. In heart failure, this helps reduce fluid overload and pulmonary congestion. While improved cardiac function may eventually lead to other positive changes such as decreased heart rate, improved blood pressure, or decreased respiratory rate, the primary indicator of furosemide's effectiveness is increased urine output.

questions.title: Cardiac Tamponade
questions.text: A client presents to the emergency department with chest pain, shortness of breath, and hypotension. Physical examination reveals distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds, and pulsus paradoxus. The nurse suspects:
questions.rationale: The classic triad of cardiac tamponade, known as Beck's triad, includes hypotension, distended neck veins (jugular venous distention), and muffled heart sounds. Pulsus paradoxus (an abnormal decrease in systolic blood pressure during inspiration) is also a key finding in cardiac tamponade. These findings occur due to fluid accumulation in the pericardial sac, which compresses the heart and reduces cardiac output. Pulmonary embolism typically presents with sudden-onset dyspnea, tachycardia, and possibly pleuritic chest pain, but not usually with muffled heart sounds or significant jugular venous distention. Aortic dissection presents with severe, tearing chest pain that often radiates to the back, but the other clinical findings mentioned are not typical. Acute myocardial infarction presents with chest pain but would not typically cause muffled heart sounds or significant jugular venous distention unless complicated by acute heart failure or rupture leading to tamponade.

questions.title: Shock Management
questions.text: A client is admitted with hypovolemic shock following trauma. Which finding would the nurse expect to observe?
questions.rationale: Decreased urine output is an expected finding in hypovolemic shock due to decreased renal perfusion secondary to decreased circulatory volume and compensatory mechanisms that shunt blood away from the kidneys to vital organs (brain and heart). Warm, flushed skin would be more consistent with early septic shock or anaphylactic shock; in hypovolemic shock, the skin is typically cool and pale due to peripheral vasoconstriction. Bradycardia is not typical of hypovolemic shock; tachycardia is expected as the heart attempts to compensate for decreased stroke volume by increasing rate. Capillary refill time is increased (delayed), not decreased, in hypovolemic shock due to peripheral vasoconstriction and decreased tissue perfusion.

questions.title: Coronary Artery Disease Risk Factors
questions.text: A nurse is conducting a risk assessment for coronary artery disease. Which risk factor is considered modifiable?
questions.rationale: Smoking is a modifiable risk factor for coronary artery disease, meaning that it can be changed through lifestyle choices or interventions. Smoking increases the risk of atherosclerosis and thrombosis, leading to coronary artery disease and acute cardiac events. Age is a non-modifiable risk factor, as the risk of coronary artery disease increases with advancing age regardless of other factors. Family history is also non-modifiable, as genetic predisposition cannot be changed. Gender is non-modifiable as well; men generally have a higher risk of coronary artery disease than women until women reach menopause, after which the risk is more similar.

questions.title: Respiratory Distress
questions.text: A client is admitted to the emergency department with acute respiratory distress. Which intervention should the nurse implement first?

choices.text: Position the client upright with arms supported on overbed table

questions.rationale: The first intervention for a client in acute respiratory distress should be positioning the client upright with arms supported on an overbed table (or similar surface). This position, often called the tripod position, maximizes lung expansion and diaphragmatic excursion, reducing the work of breathing and improving oxygenation. While bronchodilators may be essential for certain causes of respiratory distress (such as asthma or COPD exacerbation), proper positioning is always the first step to optimize respiratory function. Obtaining arterial blood gases is important for assessment but would not take priority over interventions to improve oxygenation and ventilation. Preparation for endotracheal intubation may be necessary if the client's condition deteriorates, but it would not be the first intervention unless the client is in imminent respiratory failure requiring immediate airway protection.

questions.title: Trauma Assessment
questions.text: A client arrives at the emergency department following a motor vehicle collision. During the primary survey, which assessment has the highest priority?
questions.rationale: Airway patency has the highest priority during the primary survey of a trauma patient, following the ABCDE approach (Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure). Ensuring an open airway is critical as even a brief period of airway obstruction can lead to hypoxia and brain damage. Circulatory status (assessment of pulses, bleeding, and perfusion) is the third priority after airway and breathing have been addressed. Neurological status (often assessed using the Glasgow Coma Scale or AVPU method) is part of the 'Disability' assessment, which follows after airway, breathing, and circulation have been addressed. Extremity movement would be assessed during the secondary survey, after the primary ABCDE assessment has been completed and immediate life threats have been addressed.

questions.title: Stroke Management
questions.text: A client presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of right-sided weakness and difficulty speaking. The last known well time was 2 hours ago. Which intervention is most important in the initial management?

choices.text: Obtaining a CT scan of the brain

questions.rationale: Obtaining a CT scan of the brain is the most important initial intervention for a client with suspected acute stroke. The CT scan helps differentiate between ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke, which is crucial because the treatments differ significantly. For example, thrombolytic therapy (tPA) is beneficial for ischemic stroke within a certain time window but is contraindicated in hemorrhagic stroke. Administering aspirin would be appropriate for ischemic stroke but potentially harmful for hemorrhagic stroke, so it should not be given until after CT confirmation of stroke type. Oxygen therapy should be administered only if hypoxemia is present, following the principle of maintaining normal oxygenation. Seizure precautions may be implemented but are not the most important initial intervention; seizures occur in a minority of acute stroke patients.
